title: Real gundams, top 3D generator, open-source world models, ChatGPT updates, new TTS: AI NEWS
summary: AI Search publishes a comprehensive video covering recent AI developments including 3D generation (Pixal3D, Articraft), world models (SANA-WM), open-source models (MiniCPM), video tools (Krea2, Just Dub it), and new ChatGPT features. The episode reviews over 20 emerging AI tools and products across computer vision, generative media, and productivity applications.
writeup: A weekly roundup covering seven newly released or announced open-source AI tools across video, 3D generation, image synthesis, and real-time interaction. The through-line is a wave of open releases: nearly every item covered ships code or model weights, making this a practical catalog of what practitioners can actually run now. The coverage spans dubbing via LTX 2.3 fine-tuning, pixel-space image generation that outperforms latent-space baselines, interactive world models from NVIDIA, physics-grounded video correction, and a new paradigm for real-time AI conversation that abandons turn-taking.
key_points: Just Dub It is a 2.5 GB LoRA built on LTX 2.3 that synchronizes lip movements to dubbed audio in a new language, outperforming HeyGen on sync quality according to the presenter's side-by-side tests.

Pixel3D improves image-to-3D reconstruction by explicitly mapping 2D pixels to 3D structure rather than relying on loose latent conditioning, producing higher-fidelity meshes than competitors Trellis 2 and Hunyuan3D; the released models total 24 GB and require a mid-to-high-end GPU.

Asymmetric flow models bypass the latent space and VAE entirely, generating images directly in pixel space; clever architecture changes recovered the expected compute penalty and made inference 40% faster than a naive pixel-space approach, with image quality benchmarks ranking it above latent-space competitors.

NVIDIA's Sana WM is a 2.8B parameter open-source world model trained in 15 days on 64 H100 GPUs using 200,000 labeled video clips; a distilled variant runs on a single RTX 5090 with quantization and generates a minute-long interactive clip in 34 seconds.

Phi Motion uses the physics simulator MuJoCo to score generated video: it recovers a 3D human body from the output, re-simulates it, and rewards the model for physically plausible joint angles and ground contact, substantially reducing extra or missing limbs in anatomically tricky motions like figure skating and kung fu.

Thinking Machines' interaction models replace the strict turn-taking of current voice AI with continuous multimodal input processing, letting the model notice silence, overlapping speech, interruptions, and visual cues simultaneously rather than waiting for a user to finish speaking.
